name: Dropbox MCP Server
what_it_does: MCP server enabling AI agents to interact with Dropbox cloud storage. Allows agents to upload, download, list, search, and manage files and folders in Dropbox accounts. Supports file sharing, metadata operations, and Dropbox Paper document management.
use_cases: Reading and writing files to Dropbox from AI file management agents
Searching Dropbox content for relevant documents from research agents
Automating file organization and sharing workflows in Dropbox
Integrating Dropbox file access into document processing AI pipelines
not_for: Teams using Google Drive, OneDrive, or Box (storage-platform-specific)
Large file operations exceeding Dropbox API limits
Air-gapped or on-premise file storage requirements
best_when: A developer or team uses Dropbox as their primary cloud storage and wants AI agents to read, write, and manage files there as part of automated workflows.
avoid_when: You use other cloud storage platforms. Dropbox API rate limits can be tight for bulk operations.

auth.notes: Dropbox OAuth 2.0. Requires Dropbox app registration for OAuth credentials. Scopes control access to files, sharing, account info.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Dropbox free tier: 2GB storage; API calls included
pricing.paid_tiers: Dropbox Plus/Professional/Business for more storage and features
pricing.notes: Dropbox free tier sufficient for development. MCP server is free open source.

performance.rate_limits: Dropbox API: 10,000 calls/month on free; higher on paid plans

known_agent_gotchas: Dropbox OAuth requires app registration and user authorization flow — not simple API key
10,000 API calls/month limit on free tier — can be exhausted quickly by automated agents
File path conventions in Dropbox differ from local paths — use Dropbox-style paths
Community implementation — not official Dropbox server
agent_readiness.security_notes: User files accessed via OAuth. Use minimal scopes. Files may be sensitive. Dropbox supports HIPAA at Business+ tiers.

security_score_components.security_notes: OAuth 2.0 with scopes. HTTPS. Files may contain sensitive data — use read-only scopes where possible.
